AC-1.plain_english: Write down your rules for who can access patient data and how, and make sure everyone on your team has read them.
AC-1.why_it_matters: Without written rules, every employee makes up their own, and you have no defense in a breach investigation.

AC-2.plain_english: Give every person who touches patient data their own unique login, and remove access the same day someone leaves.
AC-2.why_it_matters: If everyone shares one login, you cannot tell who accessed what when something goes wrong.

AC-3.plain_english: Set up your systems so people can only see the patient data they actually need for their job.
AC-3.why_it_matters: A billing clerk who can read therapy notes is a lawsuit waiting to happen.

AC-4.plain_english: Control how patient data moves between your systems so it cannot flow to places it should not be.
AC-4.why_it_matters: Uncontrolled data flow means patient records can end up in test environments, analytics tools, or third-party apps without anyone noticing.

AC-5.plain_english: Make sure no single person can both grant access to patient data and use that access themselves.
AC-5.why_it_matters: When one person controls everything, mistakes and misuse go undetected.

AC-6.plain_english: Give each person the minimum level of access they need to do their job, and nothing more.
AC-6.why_it_matters: The more access someone has, the more damage they can do, whether by accident or on purpose.

AC-7.plain_english: Lock accounts automatically after a few failed password attempts.
AC-7.why_it_matters: Without lockouts, attackers can try thousands of passwords until they break in.

AC-11.plain_english: Set all computers and devices to lock their screens automatically after a short period of inactivity.
AC-11.why_it_matters: An unlocked screen in a clinic or coffee shop is an open door to every patient record on that device.

AT-2.plain_english: Train every employee on how to spot phishing emails, handle patient data safely, and report security problems.
AT-2.why_it_matters: Most breaches start with a human mistake, and training is the cheapest way to prevent them.

AU-2.plain_english: Decide which events your systems must log, like logins, data access, and configuration changes, and turn on logging for all of them.
AU-2.why_it_matters: If you do not log events, you will not know a breach happened until a patient or regulator tells you.

AU-3.plain_english: Make sure every log entry records who did what, when they did it, and which patient data was involved.
AU-3.why_it_matters: Incomplete logs are as useless as no logs when investigators ask you to reconstruct what happened.

AU-6.nist_name: Audit Record Review, Analysis, and Reporting
AU-6.plain_english: Assign someone to regularly review your logs and investigate anything suspicious.
AU-6.why_it_matters: Logs that nobody reads are just wasted disk space; the average breach goes undetected for months without active review.

AU-11.plain_english: Keep your access logs for at least six years, stored safely where they cannot be tampered with.
AU-11.why_it_matters: HIPAA requires six-year document retention, and deleted logs look like you are hiding something.

AU-12.plain_english: Configure every system that handles patient data to automatically generate log entries for security-relevant events.
AU-12.why_it_matters: Systems that do not generate logs create blind spots where breaches can happen without any trace.

CA-2.plain_english: Test your security controls at least once a year to make sure they actually work the way you think they do.
CA-2.why_it_matters: Controls that are not tested regularly tend to break silently, leaving you exposed without knowing it.

CA-7.plain_english: Set up automated tools that continuously watch your systems for security problems and alert you in real time.
CA-7.why_it_matters: Annual audits catch problems a year too late; continuous monitoring catches them while you can still fix them.

CM-8.plain_english: Keep an up-to-date list of every server, database, laptop, and application that stores or processes patient data.
CM-8.why_it_matters: You cannot protect what you do not know exists, and forgotten systems are the easiest targets for attackers.

CP-1.plain_english: Write a plan for how your company will keep running if your systems go down, and make sure everyone knows where to find it.
CP-1.why_it_matters: Without a written plan, a server crash or ransomware attack turns into chaos that puts patient care at risk.

CP-2.plain_english: Create a step-by-step emergency plan that covers who does what, how to recover data, and how to keep serving patients if your main systems fail.
CP-2.why_it_matters: When systems go down, every minute of confusion is a minute patients cannot get the care they need.

CP-4.plain_english: Test your emergency recovery plan at least once a year by actually running through it, not just reading it.
CP-4.why_it_matters: A plan that has never been tested will almost certainly fail when you need it most.

CP-9.plain_english: Back up all patient data regularly, store backups in a separate location, and verify you can actually restore from them.
CP-9.why_it_matters: Ransomware or hardware failure without working backups means permanent loss of patient records.

CP-10.plain_english: Have a tested process to rebuild your systems and restore patient data to a known good state after an outage or attack.
CP-10.why_it_matters: Getting back online fast after an incident is the difference between a bad day and a business-ending event.

IA-2.plain_english: Require everyone to prove who they are before accessing any system with patient data, using strong authentication like multi-factor login.
IA-2.why_it_matters: A password alone is not enough; stolen passwords are the number one way attackers get into healthcare systems.

IA-4.plain_english: Assign a unique user ID to every person and make sure no two people ever share the same login credentials.
IA-4.why_it_matters: Shared IDs make it impossible to track who accessed a patient record, which is exactly what regulators will ask.

IA-5.plain_english: Set strong password rules, require regular password changes, and never store passwords in plain text.
IA-5.why_it_matters: Weak or reused passwords are the easiest way for attackers to break into your systems and steal patient data.

IR-1.plain_english: Write a clear incident response plan that spells out what to do, who to call, and how to report a security incident.
IR-1.why_it_matters: HIPAA requires you to report breaches within 60 days, and you cannot meet that deadline if you are figuring out the process on the fly.

IR-4.plain_english: When a security incident happens, follow a defined process to contain the damage, investigate the cause, and fix the problem.
IR-4.why_it_matters: An uncontained breach spreads fast and turns a small incident into a front-page disaster.

IR-5.plain_english: Track and document every security incident, no matter how small, so you can spot patterns and prevent repeat problems.
IR-5.why_it_matters: Small incidents that go untracked often turn out to be early warning signs of a major breach.

IR-6.plain_english: Report security incidents to the right people inside your company and to outside authorities when required by law.
IR-6.why_it_matters: Failing to report a breach to HHS on time can turn a manageable fine into a million-dollar penalty.

MA-2.plain_english: Schedule and document all maintenance on systems that handle patient data, and make sure maintenance does not expose that data.
MA-2.why_it_matters: Unplanned maintenance by unvetted people is a common way patient data gets exposed or systems get compromised.

MA-5.plain_english: Vet anyone who performs maintenance on your systems and supervise them if they are not your own employees.
MA-5.why_it_matters: A repair technician with unsupervised access to your servers has the same access as your most trusted admin.

MP-1.plain_english: Write rules for how physical media like hard drives, USB sticks, and paper records containing patient data must be handled and stored.
MP-1.why_it_matters: A lost USB drive with unencrypted patient data is a reportable breach, even if nobody ever reads it.

MP-2.plain_english: Limit who can physically access storage media that contains patient data, and keep it locked up when not in use.
MP-2.why_it_matters: An unlocked filing cabinet or an unattended external drive is just as dangerous as an unlocked database.

MP-6.plain_english: Before disposing of or reusing any device or disk that held patient data, wipe it completely so the data cannot be recovered.
MP-6.why_it_matters: Old hard drives sold on eBay with recoverable patient records have led to some of the most embarrassing HIPAA fines.

PE-1.plain_english: Write rules for who can physically enter areas where patient data is stored or processed, like server rooms and offices.
PE-1.why_it_matters: Digital security is worthless if anyone can walk into your server room and plug in a USB drive.

PE-2.plain_english: Keep a list of exactly who is authorized to enter secure areas, and update it whenever someone joins, moves, or leaves.
PE-2.why_it_matters: Former employees who still have badge access are a real and common threat to patient data.

PE-3.plain_english: Use locks, badge readers, or other controls to physically prevent unauthorized people from getting to systems with patient data.
PE-3.why_it_matters: The best firewall in the world does not help if someone can walk up to the server and pull the hard drive.

PE-5.plain_english: Place printers, fax machines, and monitors that display patient data in areas where only authorized people can see them.
PE-5.why_it_matters: Patient records left sitting on a shared printer in a hallway are a privacy violation that happens every day in healthcare.

PL-1.plain_english: Write and maintain a master security plan that documents all of your security policies, who is responsible for what, and when they get reviewed.
PL-1.why_it_matters: A complete, current security plan is the first thing auditors ask for, and not having one is an automatic finding.

PL-4.plain_english: Create an acceptable-use agreement that every employee signs, spelling out what they can and cannot do with patient data.
PL-4.why_it_matters: Without a signed agreement, you cannot hold employees accountable or discipline them for mishandling data.

PM-1.plain_english: Build a company-wide security program with clear goals, assigned responsibilities, and a timeline for getting everything done.
PM-1.why_it_matters: Scattered, one-off security efforts leave gaps that attackers will find and exploit.

PM-2.plain_english: Appoint one specific person to be in charge of your security program and give them the authority and resources to do the job.
PM-2.why_it_matters: When security is everyone's job, it ends up being nobody's job, and things fall through the cracks.

PM-9.plain_english: Define how your company identifies, evaluates, and responds to risks to patient data, and document it as a formal strategy.
PM-9.why_it_matters: Without a strategy, you will spend money on the wrong risks and miss the ones that actually threaten your patients.

PS-1.plain_english: Write rules covering how you screen, hire, manage, and terminate employees who will have access to patient data.
PS-1.why_it_matters: Insider threats cause a significant share of healthcare breaches, and good HR policies are your first line of defense.

PS-2.plain_english: Classify every job role by how much access to patient data it requires, and screen people accordingly before granting access.
PS-2.why_it_matters: Treating a database admin the same as a receptionist means either over-screening everyone or under-screening the people who matter most.

PS-3.plain_english: Run background checks on anyone who will have access to patient data before giving them that access.
PS-3.why_it_matters: Skipping background checks means you might hand the keys to patient records to someone with a history of fraud or data theft.

PS-4.plain_english: Immediately revoke all access to patient data and collect all company devices when an employee leaves or is fired.
PS-4.why_it_matters: A disgruntled former employee with active credentials is one of the most dangerous threats to your data.

PS-8.plain_english: Have a clear, documented process for disciplining employees who violate your security or privacy policies.
PS-8.why_it_matters: If there are no consequences for breaking the rules, the rules do not mean anything.

RA-2.plain_english: Classify your systems by how sensitive the patient data they handle is, so you know which ones need the strongest protection.
RA-2.why_it_matters: If you treat every system the same, you will either overspend on low-risk systems or under-protect the ones that matter most.

RA-3.plain_english: Regularly identify what could go wrong with your patient data, how likely it is, and how bad it would be, and write it all down.
RA-3.why_it_matters: HIPAA specifically requires a risk assessment, and not having one is the most common reason for enforcement action.

RA-5.plain_english: Scan your systems regularly for known security weaknesses and fix what you find on a schedule.
RA-5.why_it_matters: Attackers use automated tools to find unpatched systems; if you are not scanning, they will find your weaknesses before you do.

SA-9.plain_english: Vet every vendor and cloud service that touches patient data, get a signed Business Associate Agreement, and monitor their security.
SA-9.why_it_matters: You are legally responsible for your vendors' breaches if you did not do your due diligence and get a BAA.

SC-2.nist_name: Separation of System and User Functionality
SC-2.plain_english: Keep your administrative tools and management functions separate from the parts of your system that regular users and patients interact with.
SC-2.why_it_matters: If admin functions are mixed with user-facing features, an attacker who compromises one gets access to everything.

SC-8.plain_english: Encrypt all patient data whenever it is sent over a network, whether by email, API, or file transfer.
SC-8.why_it_matters: Unencrypted data in transit can be intercepted by anyone on the same network, including on public Wi-Fi.

SC-13.plain_english: Use strong, industry-standard encryption algorithms for protecting patient data, and do not try to build your own.
SC-13.why_it_matters: Homemade or outdated encryption is the same as no encryption when a skilled attacker targets your data.

SC-28.plain_english: Encrypt patient data wherever it is stored, including databases, file systems, backups, and laptop hard drives.
SC-28.why_it_matters: A stolen laptop or breached database with encrypted data is an incident; without encryption, it is a reportable breach.

SI-3.plain_english: Install and keep updated antivirus and anti-malware tools on every device that accesses patient data.
SI-3.why_it_matters: Ransomware targeting healthcare is surging, and outdated malware protection is like leaving your front door open.

SI-7.plain_english: Use tools to detect unauthorized changes to your software, configurations, and patient data so you know if something has been tampered with.
SI-7.why_it_matters: If an attacker quietly changes your code or data and you cannot detect it, you may be serving corrupted records for months.

SI-12.plain_english: Only keep patient data as long as you legally need it, and securely destroy it when that time is up.
SI-12.why_it_matters: Data you no longer need but still store is just more fuel for a breach, with zero business benefit.
